title: Akamai Guardicore Lateral Movement Between Segments (`guardicore:network`)
spl:
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:network" earliest=-24h
| where src_segment!=dst_segment
| stats count by src_segment, dst_segment, process_name
| where count>=20
| sort -count
description: Cross-segment flows that bypass intended choke points warrant investigation even when not yet blocked.
value: Security teams identify unauthorized cross-segment communication that bypasses intended choke points, catching lateral movement attempts even when traffic is not yet blocked by enforcement policies.
implementation: Install the add-on, configure API polling intervals appropriate to API limits, and map assets to CMDB for enrichment lookups.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count

references.title: Splunkbase — Akamai Guardicore Add-on for Splunk

knownFalsePositives: Microseg blocks from new app deployments, missing exception rules, or planned segmentation rollouts.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for any computer inside our network that starts talking to systems in a different part of the network where it normally would not, because that can mean an intruder is moving around.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Akamai Guardicore Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7426) v3.x installed and collecting network flow events from Centra via REST API or syslog.
• Data in `index=microseg` with `sourcetype=guardicore:network`. Key fields: `src_ip`, `dst_ip`, `src_segment`, `dst_segment`, `src_asset`, `dst_asset`, `protocol`, `dst_port`, `process_name`, `bytes_sent`, `bytes_received`, `action`.
• Guardicore agents deployed on assets in at least two distinct segments. The Reveal feature must be active to capture cross-segment flows.
• Create a `gc_segments.csv` lookup with `segment_name,environment,owner_team,criticality` and a `gc_approved_flows.csv` lookup: `src_segment,dst_segment,dst_port,protocol,approved` for baseline approved cross-segment communication.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify cross-segment flows are captured:
```spl
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:network" earliest=-24h
| where src_segment!=dst_segment
| stats count dc(src_ip) as src_hosts dc(dst_ip) as dst_hosts by src_segment, dst_segment
| sort - count
```
This shows all segment-to-segment traffic. Healthy networks have well-defined patterns (e.g., app→db, web→app). Unexpected pairs warrant investigation.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Unapproved cross-segment flows:
```spl
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:network" earliest=-24h
| where src_segment!=dst_segment
| stats count as flows sum(bytes_sent) as bytes dc(dst_port) as unique_ports values(dst_port) as ports values(process_name) as processes by src_ip, src_segment, dst_ip, dst_segment
| lookup gc_approved_flows.csv src_segment dst_segment OUTPUT approved
| where approved!="yes" OR isnull(approved)
| lookup gc_segments.csv segment_name as dst_segment OUTPUT criticality
| eval risk=case(criticality=="critical" AND unique_ports>3, "HIGH", criticality=="critical", "MEDIUM", unique_ports>5, "MEDIUM", 1==1, "LOW")
| sort - risk, - flows
```

Understanding this SPL: Filters to cross-segment traffic, then excludes approved flows using the baseline lookup. Remaining flows are potential lateral movement. A source IP connecting to multiple ports on a destination in a different segment is a classic reconnaissance pattern (port scanning). The `processes` field reveals the tool — `ssh`, `rdp`, `psexec`, or unexpected binaries like `mimikatz.exe` are high-fidelity indicators.

First-seen cross-segment communication:
```spl
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:network" earliest=-24h
| where src_segment!=dst_segment
| eval pair=src_segment."→".dst_segment.":".dst_port
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen count by pair
| where first_seen > relative_time(now(), "-24h")
| sort first_seen
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Guardicore Centra: Reveal → Network Map → select a segment pair → view the connection list. Compare flows with Splunk.
(b) Perform a controlled test: from a host in segment A, SSH to a host in segment B. Verify the cross-segment flow appears in Splunk with the correct `src_segment`, `dst_segment`, and `process_name=sshd`.
(c) Validate the approved flows lookup: ensure common legitimate flows (monitoring tools, backup agents, AD authentication) are listed to reduce alert noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Guardicore — Lateral Movement Detection"):
• Row 1 — Single-value: "Unapproved cross-segment flows (24h)", "New segment pairs detected".
• Row 2 — Network diagram (if available) or Sankey: unapproved flow paths.
• Row 3 — Table: src IP/segment, dst IP/segment, ports, processes, flow count, risk.

Alert: Trigger when unapproved cross-segment traffic reaches a critical-tagged segment, or when a new segment pair not seen in the last 30 days appears. Route to SOC for immediate triage.

Runbook:
1. **Known application newly crossing segments** — Application team deployed to a new segment without updating the policy. Add to `gc_approved_flows.csv` and create an allow rule in Centra.
2. **Unknown process crossing segments** — Potential compromise. Isolate the source host in Centra. Investigate with EDR: check process lineage, check if the binary is signed, check for persistence mechanisms.
3. **Port scan pattern (many ports to one destination)** — Attacker reconnaissance. Block at the Centra policy level immediately. Investigate the source host for compromise.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Too many unapproved flows** — The `gc_approved_flows.csv` lookup is incomplete. Run the search without the lookup filter for 7 days and populate the baseline with legitimate flows.
• **`src_segment` or `dst_segment` showing as raw IP** — The asset is not labeled in Centra. See UC-17.6.8.
• **No cross-segment events** — All assets may be in the same segment, or the agent is not capturing network flows. Check Centra → Assets → verify agents are healthy.
